findings.description: Symlink target from getLinkName() flows to SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink() without canonical path validation - allows writing outside extraction directory
findings.reasoning: The extractFile() method validates the entry name via canonical path check (lines 347-353), but does NOT validate the symlinkDestination parameter. The symlinkDestination comes directly from TarArchiveEntry.getLinkName() (set in TarUnArchiver.java:118), which is the symlink target as stored in the archive. An attacker-controlled archive can set a symlink's link name to a path like '../../etc/cron.d/evil' or an absolute path like '/etc/passwd'. Since there is no canonical path check on the symlink target before calling SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(), the symlink will be created pointing outside the extraction directory. This enables writing to arbitrary filesystem locations by: (1) extracting a symlink pointing outside the directory, then (2) extracting a file with a name that traverses through the symlink to write at the target location.

evidence.code_context: TarUnArchiver.java:118 - final String symlinkDestination = te.isSymbolicLink() ? te.getLinkName() : null; AbstractUnArchiver.java:342 - final File targetFileName = FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName); AbstractUnArchiver.java:347-353 - Canonical path check on entryName - PROTECTED. AbstractUnArchiver.java:371 - SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(targetFileName, new File(symlinkDestination)) - NO validation of symlinkDestination.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: getLinkName() [TarUnArchiver.java:118] -> symlinkDestination parameter [AbstractUnArchiver.java:330] -> new File(symlinkDestination) [AbstractUnArchiver.java:371] -> SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(). The canonical path check at lines 347-353 only checks the extracted file location (entryName), not the symlink target.
evidence.notes: The canonical path check only checks entryName but not symlinkDestination. Attacker controls both the symlink name (which IS checked) and the symlink target (which is NOT checked).

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a malicious tar archive with a symlink entry whose link target points outside the extraction directory, then a file entry that traverses through the symlink to write arbitrary content to system paths
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker can supply a malicious tar archive for extraction by TarUnArchiver
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal via symlink (Zip Slip variant)

findings.description: Archive entry name from f.getName() flows to file copy operation in DirectoryArchiver without canonical path containment check
findings.reasoning: In DirectoryArchiver.execute(), fileName is obtained from f.getName() (an archive entry name) at line 91, then destDir is obtained as the canonical path of the destination directory. The resulting fileName is constructed as a string concatenation: destDir + File.separator + fileName. However, there is no subsequent canonical path check to verify the resulting path stays within destDir. The string 'destDir + / + ../../etc/passwd' is passed to copyFile() which constructs 'new File(vPath)' - this will resolve to a path OUTSIDE destDir. Unlike AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() which performs a proper canonical check, DirectoryArchiver.execute() has no such protection. An archive entry named '../../etc/cron.d/evil' would cause files to be written outside the intended destination directory.

evidence.code_context: DirectoryArchiver.java lines 91-99: String fileName = f.getName(); final String destDir = destDirectory.getCanonicalPath(); fileName = destDir + File.separator + fileName; No canonical path check follows. copyFile(f, fileName) is called which does new File(vPath) with no containment check. Compare to AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() which does: Path canonicalDirPath = dir.getCanonicalFile().toPath(); Path canonicalDestPath = targetFileName.getCanonicalFile().toPath(); if (!canonicalDestPath.startsWith(canonicalDirPath)) throw ArchiverException - this protection is absent in DirectoryArchiver.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: AbstractArchiver.getResources() -> ArchiveEntry.getName() -> fileName = destDir + separator + fileName -> copyFile(f, fileName) -> new File(vPath). The archive entry name can contain ../ sequences that are not filtered.
evidence.notes: The destDir is canonical (via getCanonicalPath()) but the full path is never re-canonicalized and checked. String concatenation 'destDir + / + ../foo' does NOT resolve to destDir/foo but is an unparsed string that resolves upward when used as a file path.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies an archive entry with a name containing path traversal sequences (../../) that causes DirectoryArchiver to write files outside the intended destination directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker can control archive entry names fed to DirectoryArchiver, e.g. via addArchivedFileSet with a malicious source archive

findings.description: Symlink target from SymlinkDestinationSupplier.getSymlinkDestination() flows to SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink() without canonical path validation in DirectoryArchiver
findings.reasoning: In DirectoryArchiver.execute(), when the resource is a SymlinkDestinationSupplier, the symlink destination (target) is obtained without any canonical path validation. The symlink is created at 'fileName' (which is destDir + / + archiveEntryName), and it points to 'dest' (the symlink destination from the archive), which becomes 'new File(dest)'. Neither the symlink location nor the symlink target is validated against the destination directory boundary. This is identical to the AbstractUnArchiver symlink vulnerability - an attacker can create a symlink that points to arbitrary filesystem locations outside the extraction directory.

evidence.code_context: DirectoryArchiver.java lines 94-101: if (resource instanceof SymlinkDestinationSupplier) { String dest = ((SymlinkDestinationSupplier) resource).getSymlinkDestination(); File target = new File(dest); File symlink = new File(fileName); makeParentDirectories(symlink); SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(symlink, target); } No canonical path validation on 'dest' (symlink target) before creating the symlink.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: SymlinkDestinationSupplier.getSymlinkDestination() -> dest -> new File(dest) -> SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(symlink, target). No validation that target stays within destDir.
evidence.notes: The symlink target 'dest' can be an absolute path like /etc/passwd or relative traversal like ../../etc/cron.d - no check is performed.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a resource implementing SymlinkDestinationSupplier with a symlink target pointing outside the destination directory, enabling arbitrary file writes through the symlink
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker can control archive entries fed to DirectoryArchiver including symlink destination

findings.description: Zip symlink target from getUnixSymlink() flows to SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink() without canonical path validation in AbstractZipUnArchiver
findings.code_snippet:
extractFile( getSourceFile(), outputDirectory, cis,
                                     ze.getName(), new Date( ze.getTime() ), ze.isDirectory(),
                                     ze.getUnixMode() != 0 ? ze.getUnixMode() : null,
                                     resolveSymlink( zipFile, ze ), getFileMappers() );
findings.reasoning: The resolveSymlink() method at line 168 returns zf.getUnixSymlink(ze) - the symlink target directly from the zip archive entry. This is attacker-controlled. It flows into extractFile() as the symlinkDestination parameter, which at line 371 of AbstractUnArchiver is used directly in SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(targetFileName, new File(symlinkDestination)) without any canonical path check. While the entry name (ze.getName()) is validated against the output directory via canonical path check, the symlink TARGET is not. An attacker can create a zip file with a Unix symlink entry pointing to an arbitrary location (e.g., /etc/passwd or ../../etc/cron.d), causing the symlink to be created pointing outside the extraction directory.

evidence.code_context: AbstractZipUnArchiver.java:168-179: resolveSymlink returns zf.getUnixSymlink(ze) directly from zip archive. AbstractZipUnArchiver.java:207-209: resolveSymlink result passed to extractFile() as symlinkDestination. AbstractUnArchiver.java:371: SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(targetFileName, new File(symlinkDestination)) - no validation of symlinkDestination.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: ZipArchiveEntry (attacker-controlled) -> getUnixSymlink(ze) [AbstractZipUnArchiver.java:174] -> resolveSymlink return value -> symlinkDestination param [AbstractUnArchiver.java:330] -> new File(symlinkDestination) [AbstractUnArchiver.java:371] -> SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink()
evidence.notes: Same symlink path traversal vulnerability as TarUnArchiver but for zip files. The canonical check at lines 347-353 only validates the entry name location, not the symlink target.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a malicious zip archive with a Unix symlink entry whose target points outside the extraction directory, enabling arbitrary filesystem writes
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker can supply a malicious zip archive for extraction by AbstractZipUnArchiver
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal via symlink (Zip Slip variant)

findings.description: Archive entry name flows to new File(vPath) in DirectoryArchiver.copyFile() without canonical path containment check - path traversal
findings.code_snippet: final File outFile = new File( vPath );
findings.reasoning: This is the sink for the path traversal in DirectoryArchiver. The vPath argument comes from execute() where fileName = destDir + File.separator + f.getName(). While destDir is canonical, the complete path string (including the archive entry name with potential ../ sequences) is never re-canonicalized. new File(vPath) accepts the raw string, and when the file is subsequently written (via ResourceUtils.copyFile), the OS resolves ../ sequences, allowing writes outside the intended destination directory. This is a direct consequence of the string-based path construction at line 99 without canonical path validation.

evidence.code_context: DirectoryArchiver.copyFile() at line 128-138: protected void copyFile(final ArchiveEntry entry, final String vPath) { if (vPath.length() <= 0) return; final PlexusIoResource in = entry.getResource(); final File outFile = new File(vPath); ... ResourceUtils.copyFile(entry.getInputStream(), outFile); vPath contains destDir + '/' + entryName where entryName can contain ../.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: ArchiveEntry.getName() [containing ../] -> fileName at DirectoryArchiver:91 -> fileName = destDir + sep + fileName at line 99 -> copyFile(f, fileName) -> new File(vPath) at line 138. No canonical path check performed.
evidence.notes: This is the file write sink corresponding to the path construction vulnerability at line 99.

exploit_payload.scenario: Archive entry with path traversal sequence in name causes DirectoryArchiver to write files outside intended destination directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker can control archive entry names processed by DirectoryArchiver

findings.description: TarUnArchiver entry name flows to extractFile() which has canonical path validation - false positive for entry name traversal
findings.code_snippet:
extractFile( sourceFile, destDirectory, tis, te.getName(), te.getModTime(), te.isDirectory(),
                            te.getMode() != 0 ? te.getMode() : null, symlinkDestination, fileMappers );
findings.reasoning: The tar entry name te.getName() flows to extractFile() in AbstractUnArchiver, which performs a proper canonical path check before writing (lines 347-353): Path canonicalDirPath = dir.getCanonicalFile().toPath(); Path canonicalDestPath = targetFileName.getCanonicalFile().toPath(); if (!canonicalDestPath.startsWith(canonicalDirPath)) throw ArchiverException. This is a sound canonicalize-then-check pattern using java.nio.file.Path.startsWith() which is component-aware. The entry name traversal is properly blocked. NOTE: The symlink destination (symlinkDestination = te.getLinkName()) is a separate vulnerability already filed.

evidence.code_context: AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile() lines 342-353: final File targetFileName = FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName); Path canonicalDirPath = dir.getCanonicalFile().toPath(); Path canonicalDestPath = targetFileName.getCanonicalFile().toPath(); if (!canonicalDestPath.startsWith(canonicalDirPath)) { throw new ArchiverException(Entry is outside of target directory); }
evidence.dataflow_analysis: te.getName() -> extractFile() -> FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) -> getCanonicalFile().toPath() -> Path.startsWith() check. The check is BEFORE any file write operation.
evidence.notes: This is a sound canonicalize-then-check pattern. Path.startsWith() on java.nio.file.Path is component-aware, not string prefix. The vulnerability in this file is the symlink destination (getLinkName()) not the entry name.

findings.description: AbstractZipArchiver lines 422/426 are in archive creation context (reading source files to add to zip), not extraction - no path traversal risk for destination writes
findings.reasoning: This code is in AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs() which is called during archive CREATION (building a zip file), not extraction. The File object 'f = new File(dir)' is used to read resource attributes of the directory being added to the archive. The 'dir' string comes from addedDirs.asStringDeque(entry) which tracks parent directory paths of entries being added. This is NOT writing extracted content to the filesystem - it is reading source directories to include in the zip. The path traversal concern here is about reading files during archive creation, which has a much lower impact and is a different scenario than Zip Slip during extraction. Additionally, this is a build tool context where the source paths are typically controlled by build scripts.

evidence.code_context: AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs() at lines 406-431: This method adds parent directory entries to a zip file being created. 'dir' comes from addedDirs.asStringDeque(entry) - a deque of parent directory path strings derived from archive entry names during zip creation. new File(dir) reads attributes for the PlexusIoResource to be zipped, not for extraction. zipDir() writes a directory entry TO the zip output stream.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: ArchiveEntry.getName() -> addedDirs.asStringDeque() -> dir string -> new File(dir) -> AnonymousResource -> zipDir() writes TO zip output. This is archive creation, not extraction.
evidence.notes: False positive - this is archive creation code. The sink is writing to the zip output stream (ConcurrentJarCreator zOut), not to the filesystem as extracted content.

findings.description: AbstractZipArchiver line 426 - archive creation context, not extraction - false positive for path traversal to arbitrary filesystem write
findings.code_snippet: zipDir( res, zOut, dir, archiveEntry.getDefaultDirMode(), encoding );
findings.reasoning: Same as line 422 - this is in archive creation context (AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs()). The zipDir() call writes a directory entry to the zip output stream (ConcurrentJarCreator zOut), not to the filesystem. Archive entry names flow to zip output, not to file system extraction. This is false positive - the path traversal concern applies to extraction (unarchiving), not to zip file creation where the output is a zip stream.

evidence.code_context: AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs() - this is zip creation code. zipDir() writes to ConcurrentJarCreator zOut (the zip output stream being built), not to the filesystem. The archive entry name flows into the zip being created, not to a file being extracted.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: ArchiveEntry.getName() -> dir string -> zipDir(res, zOut, dir, ...) -> writes directory entry to zip output stream. Output is the zip file being created, not an extracted file.
evidence.notes: False positive - archive creation context. The vulnerable pattern (entry name to filesystem write without check) does not apply here.

findings.description: Symlink destination not validated against destination directory during archive extraction - allows path traversal via symlink
findings.reasoning: The extractFile method performs a canonical path check to ensure the symlink *file* (targetFileName) is within the destination directory. However, the `symlinkDestination` (where the symlink *points to*) is never validated against the destination directory. An attacker can craft a malicious archive (tar, zip) containing a symlink entry whose link target is an absolute path like `/etc/passwd` or a relative path like `../../../../etc/shadow`. When extracted, the symlink file will be within the destination directory (passing the check), but the symlink will point to an arbitrary location outside it. Any subsequent read or write through the symlink will then access files outside the intended destination. The `symlinkDestination` comes directly from the archive entry (e.g., `te.getLinkName()` in TarUnArchiver), which is fully attacker-controlled.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: TarUnArchiver: te.getLinkName() -> symlinkDestination -> extractFile() -> new File(symlinkDestination) [no validation]
evidence.notes: The canonical path check validates WHERE the symlink is placed (must be inside destDir), but NOT where the symlink POINTS TO. This allows a symlink to point to /etc/passwd or ../../../../etc/shadow.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a malicious tar/zip archive containing a symlink entry whose link target points to sensitive files (e.g., /etc/passwd) outside the extraction directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker can control the contents of an archive file that is processed by TarUnArchiver or AbstractZipUnArchiver
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal via symlink allowing arbitrary file read/write

findings.description: Path traversal in DotDirectiveArchiveFinalizer - directory path from dot file content not validated for traversal sequences
findings.reasoning: The DotDirectiveArchiveFinalizer reads dot files from dotFileDirectory and parses their content. Lines from these files are split on ":" and s[0] is used to construct a directory path as `new File(dotFileDirectory, s[0])`. If the dot file content contains path traversal sequences like `../../etc`, the resulting directory would escape the intended dotFileDirectory. However, the dotFileDirectory itself is set by the application and the dot files are read from it (so the attacker would need to be able to write to the dotFileDirectory or supply malicious dot file content). The exploitability depends on how dotFileDirectory is configured and whether an attacker can influence the dot file content. This is a manual review item to check whether the dot file source is attacker-controlled.

evidence.notes: s[0] from dot file content is used directly in File construction without sanitization. If dot file content is attacker-controlled (e.g., via file write in dotFileDirectory), traversal is possible. This is an archiver side issue (not extraction), so impact is limited to what files get included in the archive.

findings.description: File construction in ArchiveEntry.createSymlinkEntry - server-side archiver creation, not an extraction vulnerability
findings.code_snippet: symlinkName, new PlexusIoVirtualSymlinkResource( new File( symlinkName ), symlinkDestination ), SYMLINK,
findings.reasoning: This is in the archiver (creation) side, not the unarchiver (extraction) side. The `symlinkName` parameter is used to create a PlexusIoVirtualSymlinkResource as part of building an archive, not for filesystem extraction. The `symlinkName` would be an archive entry name being added to an archive, and callers (AbstractArchiver.addSymlink) are expected to be server-side code. This is not a path traversal vulnerability in the extraction sense.

evidence.notes: This is archive creation (archiver), not extraction (unarchiver). The File is created to represent a virtual symlink resource in the archive, not to extract to filesystem. Server-controlled input.

findings.description: File constructions in DirectoryArchiver.execute - archiver side with server-controlled input, not extraction vulnerability
findings.code_snippet:
fileName = destDir + File.separator + fileName;
                PlexusIoResource resource = f.getResource();
findings.reasoning: DirectoryArchiver is an archiver (copies files to a directory), not an unarchiver. The `fileName` is constructed from `f.getName()` which comes from internal `ArchiveEntry` objects added to the archiver via the API. These entries are populated by server-side code, not by attacker-controlled archive contents. The `destDir` is the canonical path of the configured destination directory. While `f.getName()` could theoretically contain traversal sequences, the ArchiveEntry names are set by the application, not read from untrusted archive files. This is not an extraction path traversal.

evidence.notes: DirectoryArchiver is a file-copying archiver. Entries come from server-side addFile/addFileSet API calls, not from untrusted archive contents. No path traversal risk from external input.

findings.description: File constructions in DirectoryArchiver.copyFile - vPath is server-controlled canonical path concatenation
findings.code_snippet: final File outFile = new File( vPath );
findings.reasoning: The `copyFile` method receives `vPath` which is constructed in the calling `execute()` method as `destDir + File.separator + fileName`, where `destDir` is the canonical path of the destination directory and `fileName` is from an internal ArchiveEntry (server-controlled). This is an archiving utility, not an extraction path, so no attacker-controlled input flows into this File construction.

evidence.notes: vPath is constructed server-side from canonical destination path + entry name. Entry names come from server-side API calls, not untrusted archive content.

findings.description: File constructions in JarArchiver.findJarName and grabFilesAndDirs - server-side classpath/file resolution, not extraction vulnerability
findings.reasoning: JarArchiver.findJarName constructs a File purely to extract its `.getName()` component (basename). This is a benign filename normalization operation on server-side classpath entries, not a filesystem operation that could allow path traversal. The file is never created/read/written - only its name component is extracted. Similarly, grabFilesAndDirs at line 783 opens a ZipFile from a server-configured path string. Both are on the archiver (creation) side with server-controlled inputs.

evidence.notes: findJarName only calls .getName() to get the filename component - no filesystem operations on the constructed File. grabFilesAndDirs uses server-configured paths to read JARs for index creation.

findings.description: File construction in JarArchiver.grabFilesAndDirs - server-side file path, not extraction vulnerability
findings.reasoning: The `file` parameter in grabFilesAndDirs is a path to a JAR file that is part of the server-configured classpath for JAR index creation. This is not attacker-controlled archive content being extracted, but rather a server-configured path used during archive creation. No path traversal vulnerability from external input.

evidence.notes: Server-configured classpath entry, not attacker-controlled archive content.

findings.description: File constructions in AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs - server-side archiver directory creation, not extraction vulnerability
findings.reasoning: AbstractZipArchiver.addParentDirs constructs File objects from `dir` (a directory name popped from a deque of directories being added to a zip archive) and optionally `baseDir`. These are used on the archiver (creation) side to create PlexusIoResource entries for the zip. The `dir` comes from an internal deque of directories computed from archive entry paths that were added to the archiver via server-side API calls (not from untrusted archive extraction). This is not an extraction path traversal.

evidence.notes: Archiver (zip creation) side only. The dir values come from internal addedDirs tracking of directories already added to the archive via server-side API. Not extraction of untrusted content.

findings.description: File construction in TarUnArchiver.execute(String path, File outputDirectory) - simple path-to-File conversion, not a path traversal vulnerability
findings.code_snippet: execute( new File( path ), getDestDirectory(), getFileMappers() );
findings.reasoning: This is in the `execute(String path, File outputDirectory)` override method which simply converts a server-configured source file path string to a File object before calling the main execute method. The `path` parameter represents the source archive file path (set by the application/build system), not an archive entry name from untrusted content. This is not an extraction path traversal - the actual path traversal protection happens within the called `execute(File, File, FileMapper[])` method which processes individual tar entries and calls `extractFile()` where the canonical path check occurs. The File construction here is for the source archive file itself, which is server-controlled.

evidence.notes: The path here is the source TAR archive file path configured by the server/build system. This is not an archive entry name from untrusted content. The actual extraction with path traversal protection happens in the delegated execute() method.

findings.description: Symlink target from TAR archive entry (getLinkName) flows to file operation without path traversal validation - allows symlink pointing outside destination directory
findings.reasoning:
The method extractFile() validates that targetFileName (the symlink file itself) is within the destination directory using canonical path checks (lines 347-352). However, the symlinkDestination parameter - which comes directly from te.getLinkName() in TarUnArchiver.java line 118 (untrusted archive content) - is never validated before being used as the symlink target at line 371.

An attacker can craft a malicious TAR archive containing a symlink entry whose getLinkName() returns a path like "../../../../etc/passwd" or "/etc/shadow". When extracted, the created symlink will point outside the destination directory. Any application code that subsequently reads files through the extracted archive tree will follow the symlink to the attacker-controlled destination, achieving path traversal.

The canonical path check only protects the symlink location (where the symlink file is created), not where it points. This is a fundamental gap: the symlink itself stays inside the dest dir, but it points outside of it.

Data flow:
1. TarUnArchiver.java:118 - `te.getLinkName()` returns attacker-controlled symlink target
2. TarUnArchiver.java:120 - passed as `symlinkDestination` to extractFile()
3. AbstractUnArchiver.java:330 - received as parameter
4. AbstractUnArchiver.java:371 - used directly in `new File(symlinkDestination)` without validation

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: te.getLinkName() at TarUnArchiver.java:118 -> symlinkDestination variable -> passed to extractFile() -> AbstractUnArchiver.java:371 new File(symlinkDestination). No validation of symlink target path exists in this chain.
evidence.notes: The canonical path check before the symlink creation only validates 'targetFileName' (symlink location), not 'symlinkDestination' (symlink target). The same shared extractFile() method is used from both TAR and ZIP unarchiver flows, so ZIP archives with Unix symlinks would be similarly affected.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious TAR archive with a symlink entry whose link target is '../../../../etc/passwd'; when extracted, the symlink inside the destination directory points to arbitrary filesystem paths, allowing reads of sensitive files
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The attacker must be able to supply a TAR archive for extraction by a system using plexus-archiver (e.g., Maven build that processes user-supplied archives)

findings.description: Symlink target from ZIP archive entry (getUnixSymlink) flows to file operation without path traversal validation - allows symlink pointing outside destination directory
findings.code_snippet:
extractFile( getSourceFile(), outputDirectory, cis,
                                     ze.getName(), new Date( ze.getTime() ), ze.isDirectory(),
                                     ze.getUnixMode() != 0 ? ze.getUnixMode() : null,
                                     resolveSymlink( zipFile, ze ), getFileMappers() );
findings.reasoning:
The AbstractZipUnArchiver.execute() method calls resolveSymlink(zipFile, ze) which returns zf.getUnixSymlink(ze) - the raw symlink target from the ZIP archive entry. This untrusted value is passed as the symlinkDestination parameter to the shared extractFile() method.

In extractFile() (AbstractUnArchiver.java:369-371), the symlinkDestination is used without any path containment check:
  if (!StringUtils.isEmpty(symlinkDestination)) {
    SymlinkUtils.createSymbolicLink(targetFileName, new File(symlinkDestination));
  }

The canonical path check at lines 347-352 only validates the symlink file location (targetFileName), NOT the symlink target (symlinkDestination). An attacker can embed a Unix symlink in a ZIP archive with a target like '../../../../etc/shadow', and when extracted, the symlink will point outside the destination directory.

Data flow:
1. AbstractZipUnArchiver.java:173 - zf.getUnixSymlink(ze) reads symlink target from ZIP
2. AbstractZipUnArchiver.java:209 - passed as resolveSymlink(zipFile, ze) to extractFile()
3. AbstractUnArchiver.java:330 - received as symlinkDestination parameter
4. AbstractUnArchiver.java:371 - used in new File(symlinkDestination) without validation

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: zf.getUnixSymlink(ze) in resolveSymlink() at AbstractZipUnArchiver.java:173 -> passed to extractFile() -> AbstractUnArchiver.java:371 new File(symlinkDestination). No validation of symlink target path exists.
evidence.notes: This is the same vulnerability as in TarUnArchiver but via the ZIP code path. Both flow to the same unprotected sink in AbstractUnArchiver.extractFile().

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious ZIP archive with a Unix symlink entry whose target is '../../../../etc/shadow'; upon extraction the symlink inside the destination directory points to arbitrary filesystem paths
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The attacker must be able to supply a ZIP archive for extraction by a system using plexus-archiver with Unix symlink support enabled
